name: Domain-Driven Design
description: Adherence to Domain-Driven Design tactical and strategic patterns from Eric Evans and Vaughn Vernon. Bounded contexts, aggregates, repositories, and a ubiquitous language let the code reflect the real domain rather than incidental implementation details.

principles.name: Ubiquitous Language
principles.description: The codebase must reflect the language of the business domain, shared between developers and domain experts

requirements.text: Class, method, and variable names must use domain terminology
requirements.description: Code identifiers should match the vocabulary used by domain experts — not technical jargon, database column names, or generic terms like 'data', 'info', 'manager'

requirements.text: No translation layers between domain concepts and code names
requirements.description: If the business calls it an 'Invoice', the code must call it 'Invoice' — not 'BillingRecord', 'PaymentDocument', or 'bill_tbl'

requirements.text: Domain operations named as verbs from the business process
requirements.description: Methods like 'placeOrder', 'approveApplication', 'cancelReservation' — not 'processData', 'handleRequest', 'doAction'

requirements.text: Avoid ambiguous or overloaded terms across bounded contexts
requirements.description: If 'Account' means different things in billing vs authentication, each context must have its own explicit model — no shared ambiguous types

requirements.text: No generic suffixes that obscure domain meaning
requirements.description: Classes named 'OrderManager', 'UserHelper', 'DataProcessor', 'PaymentHandler' signal missing domain language — name them after what they actually do in the domain (e.g., 'OrderFulfillmentService', 'PricingPolicy')

requirements.text: Boolean methods and properties express domain predicates
requirements.description: 'isOverdue()', 'canBeShipped()', 'hasReachedCreditLimit()' — not 'checkStatus()', 'getFlag()', 'isValid()' without context

requirements.text: Enums and constants represent domain concepts, not technical states
requirements.description: OrderStatus.AWAITING_PAYMENT, ShipmentState.IN_TRANSIT — not Status.ACTIVE, State.STEP_2, Type.TYPE_A

requirements.text: Exceptions describe domain violations, not technical failures
requirements.description: 'InsufficientFundsException', 'OrderAlreadyShippedException' — not 'InvalidStateException', 'BusinessRuleException', 'Error400'

principles.name: Bounded Contexts
principles.description: The system is divided into explicit bounded contexts, each with its own model and clear boundaries

requirements.text: Each bounded context has its own domain model with no shared mutable state
requirements.description: Contexts do not share entity classes, database tables, or domain objects directly — each owns its data and schema

requirements.text: Context boundaries are explicit in the codebase structure
requirements.description: Separate modules, packages, or services per context — not mixed domain concepts in a single module. Look for top-level directories or packages named after business capabilities (ordering/, shipping/, billing/)

requirements.text: Communication between contexts uses well-defined integration patterns
requirements.description: Contexts interact through published interfaces, events, or anti-corruption layers — not direct imports of another context's internals. Imports crossing context boundaries are a violation

requirements.text: No domain model leakage across context boundaries
requirements.description: One context's entities, value objects, or aggregates must not appear in another context's code. A shipping context importing an ordering context's Order entity is a boundary violation

requirements.text: Context mapping relationships are documented or enforced
requirements.description: Relationships between contexts (shared kernel, customer-supplier, conformist, anti-corruption layer) should be explicit in code structure or documentation

requirements.text: Shared data between contexts is exchanged through integration events or DTOs, not shared entities
requirements.description: If ordering needs customer data, it receives a lightweight DTO or event payload — it does not import the customer context's Customer entity

requirements.text: Each context has independent persistence
requirements.description: Contexts should not share database tables or schemas. If they use the same database, each context owns its own tables with no foreign keys crossing context boundaries

principles.name: Entities
principles.description: Objects defined by their identity rather than their attributes, with continuity through their lifecycle

requirements.text: Entities have a unique, immutable identity
requirements.description: Each entity must have a stable identifier (ID) that distinguishes it from all other entities, even if all other attributes are identical. The ID field must not have a setter

requirements.text: Entity equality is based on identity, not attribute values
requirements.description: Two entities with the same ID are the same entity regardless of attribute differences — equals/hashCode must be identity-based, not field-by-field comparison

requirements.text: Entities encapsulate and enforce their own business rules
requirements.description: Validation, state transitions, and invariant enforcement belong inside the entity — not in services, controllers, or external validators. If a service method checks entity state before mutating it, that logic should move into the entity

requirements.text: Entities must not expose mutable internal state directly
requirements.description: State changes happen through domain methods that enforce invariants — no public setters that bypass business rules. 'order.addLineItem(product, qty)' not 'order.setItems(list)'

requirements.text: Entity constructors enforce creation invariants
requirements.description: An entity must be valid from the moment of creation — constructors or factory methods must reject invalid initial state. No 'new Order()' followed by 'order.setCustomer()' — required fields go in the constructor

requirements.text: Entities must not be anemic data holders
requirements.description: An entity with only getters/setters and no behavior is an anemic domain model violation. Domain logic scattered in services that manipulate entity fields externally is a code smell

requirements.text: State transitions are explicit domain operations with guard clauses
requirements.description: 'order.ship()' checks that order is paid and has items — it doesn't silently accept any transition. Invalid transitions throw domain exceptions, not generic errors

requirements.text: Entities must not depend on infrastructure or persistence concerns
requirements.description: No ORM annotations, serialization decorators, database column mappings, or framework base classes on entity code. Persistence mapping belongs in the infrastructure layer

principles.name: Value Objects
principles.description: Immutable objects defined by their attributes, with no conceptual identity

requirements.text: Value objects are immutable after creation
requirements.description: No setters, no mutation methods — any change produces a new instance. 'price.withDiscount(10)' returns a new Money, does not mutate the original

requirements.text: Value object equality is based on attribute values
requirements.description: Two value objects with identical attributes are equal — equals/hashCode must compare all fields, not identity/reference. Money(100, 'USD') == Money(100, 'USD')

requirements.text: Domain concepts that lack identity must be modeled as value objects, not entities
requirements.description: Money, addresses, date ranges, email addresses, coordinates, phone numbers, measurement units — these are values, not things with lifecycles. If you wouldn't track its history, it's a value object

requirements.text: Value objects must be self-validating
requirements.description: A value object cannot exist in an invalid state — construction must fail if inputs are invalid (e.g., negative money, malformed email, end date before start date). No 'isValid()' method needed — invalid instances simply cannot exist

requirements.text: Primitive obsession must be avoided for domain concepts
requirements.description: Use value objects instead of raw strings, ints, or floats for domain concepts — 'EmailAddress' not 'string', 'Money' not 'float', 'CustomerId' not 'int'. Passing raw primitives between methods loses domain meaning and validation

requirements.text: Value objects encapsulate domain-specific operations
requirements.description: Money knows how to add, subtract, and convert currencies. DateRange knows if it overlaps another range. Don't put this logic in services that operate on raw fields

requirements.text: Value objects replace multi-field groupings that always travel together
requirements.description: If you always pass (street, city, zip, country) together, that's an Address value object. If you always pass (amount, currency), that's Money. Repeated field groups without a wrapping type signal a missing value object

principles.name: Aggregates
principles.description: Clusters of entities and value objects with a root entity that controls access and enforces consistency boundaries

requirements.text: Each aggregate has a single root entity
requirements.description: External objects may only hold references to the aggregate root — never to internal entities or value objects. If external code can reach OrderLineItem without going through Order, the aggregate boundary is broken

requirements.text: Aggregate boundaries enforce transactional consistency
requirements.description: All invariants within an aggregate are enforced in a single transaction — cross-aggregate consistency is eventual. A single database transaction must not span multiple aggregates

requirements.text: Aggregates reference other aggregates by identity only
requirements.description: An aggregate must not hold direct object references to another aggregate — use IDs to reference across aggregate boundaries. 'order.customerId' not 'order.customer' (where customer is a full aggregate)

requirements.text: Aggregates are kept small and focused
requirements.description: Large aggregates cause contention and performance issues — prefer smaller aggregates with eventual consistency between them. An aggregate with more than 3-4 entity types inside it is likely too large

requirements.text: Only the aggregate root exposes public modification methods
requirements.description: All state changes to internal entities go through the root — external code must not reach inside to modify child entities directly. 'order.removeLineItem(itemId)' not 'order.getLineItems().remove(item)'

requirements.text: Deleting an aggregate root cascades to all internal objects
requirements.description: Internal entities and value objects have no independent lifecycle — they exist only within their aggregate. Orphaned child records in the database indicate a broken aggregate boundary

requirements.text: Aggregate invariants are checked on every state change
requirements.description: After any mutation, the aggregate must verify its internal consistency rules hold — e.g., order total matches sum of line items, reservation dates don't overlap. Invariant checks must not be optional or deferred

requirements.text: Loading an aggregate loads the entire aggregate, not partial projections
requirements.description: Repositories return complete aggregates with all internal state. If you need partial data for reads, use a separate read model (CQRS) — don't break aggregate consistency by loading partial state for writes

principles.name: Domain Services
principles.description: Stateless operations that belong to the domain but don't naturally fit within an entity or value object

requirements.text: Domain services are stateless
requirements.description: A domain service must not hold mutable state between invocations — it receives inputs, performs logic, and returns results. No instance fields that accumulate state across calls

requirements.text: Domain services express domain operations that span multiple aggregates
requirements.description: Operations that don't belong to a single entity (e.g., transferring funds between accounts, matching a buyer with a seller) belong in a domain service

requirements.text: Domain services use ubiquitous language in their interface
requirements.description: Method names must be meaningful domain verbs — 'transferFunds', 'calculateShippingCost', 'matchOrder' — not 'processTransaction', 'handleData', 'execute'

requirements.text: Domain services must not contain infrastructure concerns
requirements.description: No database access, HTTP calls, or file I/O — only pure domain logic. Infrastructure is injected through interfaces. If a domain service imports a database client, it's misplaced

requirements.text: Avoid overuse of services — prefer rich domain models
requirements.description: If logic can live in an entity or value object, it should — domain services are for cross-aggregate or truly stateless operations only. A codebase with many domain services and anemic entities has inverted the model

requirements.text: Domain services are distinct from application services
requirements.description: Domain services contain business rules (pricing calculation, eligibility check). Application services orchestrate (load aggregate, call domain service, save, dispatch event). Don't mix orchestration with business logic in one class

requirements.text: Domain services operate on domain objects, not primitives or DTOs
requirements.description: A domain service receives and returns entities, value objects, or domain types — not raw strings, maps, or infrastructure DTOs. 'calculateDiscount(Order, CustomerTier)' not 'calculateDiscount(double, String)'

principles.name: Repositories
principles.description: Abstractions that provide collection-like access to aggregates, hiding persistence details from the domain

requirements.text: Repositories provide collection-semantics for aggregate access
requirements.description: The interface should feel like an in-memory collection (add, remove, findById, findBySpec) — not like a database API (executeQuery, commit, flush, getSession)

requirements.text: Only aggregate roots have repositories
requirements.description: Internal entities within an aggregate are accessed through the root — they must not have their own repository. A LineItemRepository is a violation; line items come from OrderRepository

requirements.text: Repository interfaces are defined in the domain layer
requirements.description: The domain declares what persistence operations it needs — the infrastructure layer provides the implementation. The interface file lives next to domain entities, not next to database code

requirements.text: Repositories return fully reconstituted aggregates
requirements.description: The domain receives complete aggregate objects — not partial DTOs, raw database rows, or lazy-loading proxies that leak persistence concerns. If an aggregate is returned half-loaded, the boundary is broken

requirements.text: Repository implementations must not leak persistence details into the domain
requirements.description: No ORM sessions, query builders, connection objects, or database transactions visible to domain code. The domain calls 'repository.save(order)' and doesn't know if it's SQL, NoSQL, or a file

requirements.text: Repository methods accept domain-meaningful query criteria
requirements.description: 'findByCustomerAndStatus(customerId, OrderStatus.ACTIVE)' — not 'findByQuery("SELECT * FROM orders WHERE...")'. Query criteria should be expressed in domain terms

requirements.text: Repositories must not perform business logic
requirements.description: A repository persists and retrieves — it does not validate, calculate, or enforce business rules. If the repository is checking business conditions before saving, that logic belongs in the domain

principles.name: Domain Events
principles.description: Explicit representation of something significant that happened in the domain, enabling decoupled communication

requirements.text: Significant domain state changes are captured as domain events
requirements.description: Events like 'OrderPlaced', 'PaymentReceived', 'AccountSuspended' are first-class objects — not implicit side effects buried in method bodies. If a state change triggers reactions elsewhere, it should raise an event

requirements.text: Domain events are immutable and named in past tense
requirements.description: Events represent facts that already happened — 'OrderShipped' not 'ShipOrder'. They must not be modified after creation. All fields should be final/readonly

requirements.text: Domain events contain only the data needed to describe what happened
requirements.description: Include the aggregate ID, relevant changed values, and timestamp — not entire entity graphs, infrastructure details, or data the consumer can look up themselves

requirements.text: Cross-aggregate side effects are triggered through domain events, not direct calls
requirements.description: When an order is placed, inventory adjustment happens via an event handler — not by the order aggregate directly calling inventory code. Direct cross-aggregate method calls create tight coupling

requirements.text: Domain events are part of the domain model, not infrastructure
requirements.description: Event classes live in the domain layer — the event bus/dispatcher is infrastructure, but the events themselves are domain concepts defined alongside the aggregates that emit them

requirements.text: Event handlers must not modify the emitting aggregate
requirements.description: An event handler for 'OrderPlaced' must not reach back and modify the Order that emitted it. If the Order needs to change, that's a separate command, not a handler side effect

requirements.text: Events carry enough context to be processed without additional queries when possible
requirements.description: If a handler needs the order total to proceed, include it in the event — don't force the handler to query the order aggregate. Minimize coupling between event producers and consumers

requirements.text: Domain events are distinct from integration events
requirements.description: Internal domain events (within a bounded context) may carry rich domain types. Integration events (crossing context boundaries) should carry only primitive/serializable data and be versioned for evolution

principles.name: Anti-Corruption Layer
principles.description: A translation layer that protects the domain model from external systems and legacy code

requirements.text: External system models must not pollute the domain model
requirements.description: Third-party API responses, legacy database schemas, and external DTOs must be translated at the boundary — never used directly in domain code. Importing a Stripe SDK type in your domain layer is a violation

requirements.text: Integration adapters translate between external and domain models
requirements.description: Adapter classes map external data structures to domain objects and vice versa — the domain never sees the external format. A PaymentGatewayAdapter converts Stripe responses to domain Payment objects

requirements.text: External API changes must not require domain model changes
requirements.description: If a third-party API changes its response format, only the anti-corruption layer adapters need updating. If a Stripe API change forces you to modify your Payment entity, the ACL is missing or broken

requirements.text: Legacy system integration uses explicit translation, not shared models
requirements.description: When integrating with legacy code, create adapters that translate — do not force the new domain to conform to legacy structures or naming

requirements.text: Anti-corruption layer handles data format differences and semantic mismatches
requirements.description: If the external system uses 'client' where your domain uses 'customer', or uses cents where you use Money objects, the ACL translates — the domain code never adapts to external conventions

requirements.text: Each external system integration has its own dedicated adapter
requirements.description: Don't create a generic 'ExternalServiceAdapter' that handles multiple systems — each integration gets its own adapter with its own translation logic. This prevents one external change from affecting other integrations

principles.name: Application Layer
principles.description: Thin orchestration layer that coordinates domain objects to fulfill use cases without containing business logic

requirements.text: Application services orchestrate but do not contain business rules
requirements.description: Application services coordinate entities, repositories, and domain services — business decisions live in the domain layer. If an application service has if/else logic about domain state, that logic is misplaced

requirements.text: Application services manage transactions and cross-cutting concerns
requirements.description: Transaction boundaries, authorization checks, logging, and event dispatching are application layer responsibilities — not domain layer concerns

requirements.text: Application services define use case boundaries with explicit input/output
requirements.description: Each application service method represents a use case with a clear command/query and response — not generic CRUD operations. 'placeOrder(PlaceOrderCommand)' not 'save(Order)'

requirements.text: The application layer must not expose domain internals to external consumers
requirements.description: API responses use DTOs assembled in the application layer — domain entities are never serialized directly to external clients. Returning an entity from a REST controller leaks the domain model

requirements.text: Application layer is thin — if it grows complex, logic likely belongs in the domain
requirements.description: Complex conditionals, calculations, or multi-step business rules in application services are a smell — push them into entities or domain services. An application service method longer than 10-15 lines often contains escaped domain logic

requirements.text: Commands and queries are separate concerns
requirements.description: Methods that change state (commands) should not return domain data. Methods that return data (queries) should not change state. Mixing reads and writes in a single method obscures intent and makes the system harder to reason about

requirements.text: Application services receive commands/queries, not raw HTTP or framework objects
requirements.description: The controller layer converts HTTP requests to command/query objects — the application service never sees HttpRequest, FormData, or framework types. This ensures the application layer is framework-independent
